scripts/validate.sh.findings_text:
Review findings — scripts/validate.sh: 13 total  [MISSING=4  SUGGESTION=5  NOTE=4]

[MISSING] scripts/validate.sh:1
Missing a script header comment explaining the script's purpose, accepted arguments (if any), and what it modifies.

[MISSING] scripts/validate.sh:7
The function section lacks a descriptive comment explaining its purpose and usage in the script.

[MISSING] scripts/validate.sh:11
The function ok lacks a descriptive comment explaining its purpose and typical usage context.

[MISSING] scripts/validate.sh:15
The function fail lacks a descriptive comment explaining its purpose, side effects on FAILED, and its non-exiting behavior.

[SUGGESTION] scripts/validate.sh:39
The pattern of checking executable presence and running external validation scripts could benefit from a generic comment summarizing this repeated block's intention and failure handling.

[SUGGESTION] scripts/validate.sh:64
The "Required files" loop would be clearer with a preceding comment explaining the importance of these files and that the loop checks their presence.

[SUGGESTION] scripts/validate.sh:88
Explicitly comment why compiling specific Python files is important here (e.g., to preempt syntax errors before runtime).

[SUGGESTION] scripts/validate.sh:107
The Bridget image asset check uses compgen patterns; a comment explaining the rationale for the patterns and why image presence is conditional with a "local-only" skip would aid comprehension.

[SUGGESTION] scripts/validate.sh:121
The conditional check for OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable gating bridge prompt would benefit from a comment explaining why this condition affects test execution.

[NOTE] scripts/validate.sh:76
The find command used to detect debug or backup files is somewhat specialized; clarifying why these particular filenames/extensions are considered unacceptable would improve maintainability.

[NOTE] scripts/validate.sh:93
The tool listing check via uv run python bridge.py --tools involves parsing output with multiple grep commands; a summary comment about verifying the presence of expected tools could clarify intent.

[NOTE] scripts/validate.sh:115
The call to uv run python bridge.py with a phrase "hur ser du ut?" is not self-explanatory; a comment explaining this test's role as a face trigger smoke test would improve clarity.

[NOTE] scripts/validate.sh:129
The final "Done" section aggregates all fail counts and exits non-zero on failure; though common, a brief comment stating this intent would improve readability for new maintainers.

Review findings — review_engine/severity_engine.py: 7 total  [NOTE=7]

[NOTE] review_engine/severity_engine.py
This file appropriately encapsulates its responsibility for parsing, validating, and formatting review findings and conforms to the architectural role of context building and routing within the review engine.

[NOTE] review_engine/severity_engine.py
Severity labels include MISSING which is related to comment review but is handled here for completeness; it is unclear whether mixing architecturespecific severities (ARCHITECTURE, RISK) with comment-review severities is intentional or indicates boundary overlap.

[NOTE] review_engine/severity_engine.py
The parse_findings function drops findings with unknown severity labels rather than reporting or flagging them, which may hide potential issues with contract changes; unclear whether silent omission is intentional for robustness.

[NOTE] review_engine/severity_engine.py
The severity ordering defined in SEVERITY_ORDER includes MISSING which is not part of architecture review severities per the contract, indicating the class is designed for broader review modes; this suggests the file serves multi-mode contexts beyond just architecture.

[NOTE] review_engine/severity_engine.py
The format_summary method aggregates findings across severities but does not distinguish or segregate different review modes or contracts explicitly; unclear whether this aligns fully with ADR-004 requirements for contract-driven output.

[NOTE] review_engine/severity_engine.py
Importing only standard libraries and dataclasses indicates good encapsulation; there is no coupling to higher-level application layers or service internals.

[NOTE] review_engine/severity_engine.py
The file includes a __main__ block demonstrating usage, which is self-contained and related to the module's functionality; this is consistent with module-oriented architecture.
